texts.content_text100: When we talk about trauma, we basically intend an emotional response to an event or a situation that this person finds highly stressful, threatening, or harmful.
texts.content_text101: Childhood trauma can be caused by a one-time occurrence, a car accident, a natural disaster, an assault, or an ongoing situation, physical or sexual abuse, neglect, racism, bullying. Other than those events, a child may experience adverse childhood experiences that is various stressful situations during the first 18 years of life and result to disruption of safe, stable and nurturing family relationships.
texts.content_text102: Early trauma is associated with altered function in neural pathways of the developing brain, can affect their overall development, and if untreated can carry into adulthood various symptoms and problems, including poor regulation of emotions, aggression, learning difficulties, and impaired problems, including poor regulation of emotions, aggression, learning difficulties, and impaired social interactions. Moreover, it affects the development of certain areas of the brain such as the hippocampus, which is important for memory formation leading to memory loss or repression.
texts.content_text103: Difficulties recalling details of events that occurred before or after the traumatic event. Memories of traumatic events can be fragmented, containing very few, often distorted facts such as the time, place, or people involved, so that the narrative of the experience is muddled and incomplete.
texts.content_text104: In extreme cases when the abuse is particularly cruel, the victim in order to survive uses a coping mechanism called dissociative amnesia which in extreme cases can might lead to the formation of multiple personalities. Children who have learned to dissociate as a defense mechanism may automatically dissociate during stressful situations. So their testimony in court can become a trigger resulting to stressed, inattentive, aggressive, or even dissociated behaviors. It is important to remember that not everyone who experiences a stressful event will develop trauma.
texts.content_text105: In order for attorneys to provide their clients with the most productive and effective advocacy possible, they need to recognize the prevalence of trauma. This is known as trauma informed lawyering. A trauma-informed lawyer is able to recognize trauma, is compassionate, empathetic, inspires safety. He can ease his client's anxieties by a non-judgmental and thoughtful way that doesn't minimize or disregards the child's experiences. She works on building a positive attorney client relationship based on trust, choice, and collaboration. Moreover, he adapts the litigation strategy and prevents secondary trauma. The trauma informed approach is guided by four assumptions known as the four Rs.
texts.content_text106: First, realization about trauma and how it can affect people and groups. Second, recognizing the signs of trauma. Third, responding to trauma. Fourth, resisting retraumatization. Trauma informed lawyering refers also to the avoidance of secondary trauma which is a work related one that is it affects people working and volunteering in the fields of victim services as a result of the continuous exposure to working and volunteering in the fields of victim services as a result of the continuous exposure to trauma victims and their stories. It presents with various forms most commonly as compassion fatigue, burnout, and can have the same effects as direct trauma. Not all lawyers working with trauma develop symptoms of secondary trauma. Some may draw inspiration from a victim's resilience that strengthens their own mental and emotional fortitude.
texts.content_text107: Others can experience combassion satisfaction which motivates and in turn protects against the negative effects of trauma exposure.
